title: The Maverick: Houses and banks are about to tank
full_text:
The great Aussie housing bust is gathering momentum. Over the past three months, Cotality data shows Melbourne and Sydney dwelling values deflating annually by 11 per cent and 13 per cent respectively.
While Brisbane and Perth prices are still technically climbing, they are losing steam fast. It would not surprise me if Brisbane, barely appreciating at 3 per cent, rolls over soon. Bricks and mortar in Perth could likewise shudder to a halt. Nationally, home values are shrinking at a 6 per cent annual clip, which is bound to get worse.
According to Cotality's records stretching back 46 years, there have been three similarly large peak-to-trough corrections: 1982 to 1983 (8.5 per cent), 2017 to 2019 (8.2 per cent) and 2022 to 2023 (8.1 per cent). That is based on the official monthly indices. The more precise daily data shows the 2017 to 2019 loss maxed out around 11 per cent, while 2022 to 2023 approached 10 per cent.
Melbourne has never reclaimed its March 2022 peak as marginal buyers shifted capital interstate in response to Dan Andrews' assault on prosperity down south.
Long-time readers might recall that this columnist was the only analyst predicting a big Aussie drawdown in late 2021, which materialised over the following year. True, we expected a deeper, circa 15 per cent retrenchment. What we did not anticipate was Canberra's mass, politically motivated immigration program to buy votes, which insulated the market from 4.25 percentage points of interest rate increases that would have otherwise driven it lower.
The raw numbers are breathtaking. Prime Minister Anthony Albanese has shipped in a total of 1.6 million immigrants since 2022 alone (extrapolating recent flows to June 2026).
Aussie home values did fall by 13 per cent, inflation-adjusted, but there was a sharp recovery in Sydney in particular, where property prices jumped 11 per cent over 2023 and 2024 largely as a function of the vast wave of immigrants hunting for housing.
Depending on the path of future policy, we could easily test these records. That is grim news for stock jockeys loaded to the gills with bank equities. Balance sheet growth will decelerate dramatically, provisioning for losses will jump, and these massively overvalued shares should mean-revert. None of the majors ought to trade much above 1.5 times book value in a normal world. More on that later.
There are two principal drivers of the housing malaise: Canberra's catastrophic budget and the Reserve Bank of Australia's rate rises. The bad news on the latter is that there may be more wood to chop. Our modelling suggests the RBA could be forced to tighten another two to three times.
And if the government keeps importing voters while spending unprecedented sums feathering its own nest via an ever-expanding public service, it will be incredibly difficult for Martin Place to return inflation to its mandated 2.5 per cent target.
Although Canberra claims its ban on negative gearing and increase in the maximum effective capital gains tax rate to 47 per cent will not raise much revenue, in practice, the changes touch an enormous part of the housing market. More than half of all investment properties in the existing private rental stock are negatively geared. The split between negatively geared property investors and those positively or neutrally geared was 54-46 in 2023-24 on Tax Office data. After the three recent RBA hikes, that ratio has probably climbed to about 60-40.
Since roughly 82 per cent of investors buy established rather than new dwellings, and investors account for about one-third of new mortgages, about half of new investor loans - or one-fifth of all new home loans - are secured against negatively geared, non-new assets. Canberra's raid strikes 20 per cent of the mortgage market.
Quantifying how these measures flow through to prices is tricky.
"The government's new policies are likely to weigh on prices, although estimating the economic impact of the tax changes is very difficult," says Coolabah chief macro strategist Kieran Davies. "This is because macro models do not adequately incorporate taxes, and the only comparable changes to the tax treatment of housing occurred over 40 years ago.
"One approach is to broadly equate the tax change to an increase in mortgage rates, taking a leaf from research by Trent Saunders at CBA. The estimated increase in the mortgage rate can then be combined with results from the RBA's Saunders-Tulip model of the housing market to back out a potential impact on house prices."
On this basis, we find the tax changes equate to a mortgage rate shock on negatively geared established properties of between 2 and 2.75 percentage points, depending on the investor's bracket.
"This is calculated by backing out the increase in the mortgage rate required to raise loan repayments by the same amount as the lost tax concession on Cotality-derived estimates of an average national property worth $1 million, assuming a 3.6 per cent rental rate and an average investor mortgage rate of 6.4 per cent," Davies says.
Because negatively geared investors represent one-fifth of new lending, the tax grab has whacked the market with the equivalent of a half of one percentage point of RBA tightening, or two standard moves. Applying the Saunders-Tulip framework, we estimate this should lower real house prices by 3 to 5 per cent over one year and by a cumulative 4 to 9 per cent over two.
That could shape monetary policy. While our modelling indicates the cash rate should peak at 4.75 to 5 per cent, given very high core inflation and an unemployment rate held artificially low by profligate government spending, a decline in the value of the $12.6 trillion residential property market could give the RBA pause.
Having said that, Davies cautions that "the circa 50 basis point estimate of the increase in the overall mortgage rate does not equate to an RBA cash rate increase of 50 basis points".
"This is because other channels of the transmission mechanism of monetary policy are not affected by the tax changes. In addition, there is the possibility that banks partly counter the tax changes by lowering mortgage rates for owner-occupier loans and investor loans for new homes to make up for lost revenue."
Any way you slice it, Canberra's broken promises on negative gearing and capital gains tax are wretched news for Aussie equities and superannuation fund returns, given their outsized dependency on bank stocks.
One reason the banks trade at globally anomalous multiples is the interaction between compulsory superannuation flows and the Australian Prudential Regulation Authority's performance test. With the superannuation guarantee climbing to 12 per cent in July last year, the system's $4.5 trillion in assets now dwarfs the ASX's $3.3 trillion capitalisation, and solid nominal wage growth has turbocharged inflows. Treasury itself concedes the Your Future Your Super test creates powerful incentives for funds to hug their benchmarks, converting rivers of new member money into price-insensitive, index-weight purchases of the banks that dominate the S&P/ASX 200.
Accordingly, the banks' pricing premium reflects regulatory plumbing and flow dynamics, not fundamentals. That breeds vulnerabilities if and when investors refocus on intrinsic worth. A record housing slump coinciding with a default cycle and policy changes that hammer capital gains could usher in an enduring stretch of subpar returns.
It makes, for example, zero sense for Commonwealth Bank to be trading at 3.5 times book value. With the cash rate at 4.35 per cent and the 10-year Commonwealth bond yield about 4.5 per cent, a conventional capital asset pricing model cost of equity for CBA - with a beta of about 0.9 and an equity risk premium of 5 to 6 per cent - lands at roughly 9.5 to 10.5 per cent. Against a 13.6 per cent return on equity, CBA genuinely earns a spread of only 3.5 to 4 percentage points over its cost of capital.
A multiple of 1.6 times book is roughly where NAB and Westpac sit.
Hold on to your hats ...
Fairfax Media Management Pty Limited
author: Christopher Joye
heading: Banking and Economy
